33.1: Again Grace ran and renounced the corruptor, || A nd came down on him to bring him to nothing—
33.2: And he who caused destruction [and] perdition from before him, || A nd devastated all his order.
33.3: And he stood on a lofty summit || And uttered his voice from one end of the earth to the other,
33.4: And drew to himself all those who obeyed him, || For he did not appear as [the] evil one.
33.5: But there stood a perfect virgin who was proclaiming, and calling, and saying,
33.6: “O you sons of men, return! A nd you daughters of men, come!
33.7: And forsake the ways of that corruptor and draw near to me, || A nd I will enter into you, || A nd will bring you forth from perdition,
33.8: And make yourself wise in the ways of truth, || T hat you are not destroyed nor perish.
33.9: Hear me and be redeemed! For I am telling of the Grace of God among you”: A nd by My means you will be redeemed and become blessed.
33.10: I am your Judge; A nd they who have put Me on will not be injured, || B ut they will possess the new world that is incorruptible.
33.11: My chosen ones walk in Me, || A nd I will make known My ways to them that seek Me, || A nd I will make them trust in My Name. Hallelujah!

34.1: No way is hard where there is a simple heart,
34.2: Nor is there any wound where the thoughts are upright,
34.3: Nor is there any storm in the depth of the illuminated thought.
34.4: Where one is surrounded on every side by beauty, || T here is nothing that is divided.
34.5: The likeness of what is below is that which is above, || F or everything is above. W hat is below is nothing but the imagination of those that are without knowledge.
34.6: Grace has been revealed for your salvation. Believe, and live, and be saved. Hallelujah!

35.1: In quietness He distilled the dew of the Lord on me,
35.2: And He caused the cloud of peace to rise over my head, || W hich guarded me continually;
35.3: It was to me for salvation: E verything was shaken, and they were frightened;
35.4: And there came forth from them smoke and judgment; A nd I was keeping quiet in the order of the Lord.
35.5: He was more than a shelter to me and more than a foundation.
35.6: And I was carried like a child by its mother: A nd He gave me milk, || T he dew of the Lord,
35.7: And I grew great by His bounty, || A nd rested in His perfection,
35.8: And I spread out my hands in the lifting up of my soul: A nd I was made right with the Most High, || And I was redeemed with Him. Hallelujah!

36.1: I rested in the Spirit of the Lord, || A nd the Spirit raised Me on high,
36.2: And made Me stand on My feet in the height of the Lord, || B efore His perfection and His glory, || W hile I was praising Him by the composition of His songs.
36.3: The Spirit brought Me forth before the face of the Lord, || A nd, although [the] Son of Man, || I was [also] named the Illuminate, the Son of God,
36.4: While I was praised among the praising ones, || A nd I was great among the mighty ones.
36.5: For according to the greatness of the Most High, || S o He made Me; A nd like His own newness He renewed Me, || A nd He anointed Me from His own perfection.
36.6: And I became one of His neighbors; A nd My mouth was opened like a cloud of dew,
36.7: And my heart poured out as it were a gushing stream of righteousness,
36.8: And my access to Him was in peace. A nd I was established by the Spirit of His government. Hallelujah!

37.1: I stretched out my hands to my Lord, || A nd to the Most High I raised my voice:
37.2: And I spoke with the lips of my heart, || A nd He heard me when my voice reached Him;
37.3: His answer came to me and gave me the fruits of my labors;
37.4: And it gave me rest by the grace of the Lord. Hallelujah!

38.1: I went up to the light of Truth as if into a chariot:
38.2: And the Truth took me and led me, || A nd carried me across pits and ravines; A nd from the rocks and the waves He preserved me.
38.3: And He became to me a haven of salvation, || A nd He set me on the arms of immortal life;
38.4: And He went with me and made me rest, || A nd did not permit me to wander because He was and is the Truth.
38.5: And I ran no risk, || B ecause I walked with Him; 6 And I did not error in anything because I obeyed the Truth.
38.7: For error flees away from Him and does not meet Him, || B ut the Truth proceeds in the right path.
38.8: And whatever I did not know, He made clear to me, || A ll the poisons of error, || A nd the plagues of death which they think to be sweetness.
38.9: And I saw the destroying of the destroyer, || W hen the bride who is corrupted is adorned: A nd the bridegroom who corrupts and is corrupted.
38.10: And I asked the Truth, “Who are these?” A nd He said to me, “This is the deceiver and the error:
38.11: And they are alike in the beloved and in his bride; A nd they lead astray and corrupt the whole world.
38.12: And they invite many to the banquet,
38.13: And give them to drink of the wine of their intoxication, || A nd remove their wisdom and knowledge, || A nd so they make them without intelligence;
38.14: And then they leave them; A nd then these go about like madmen corrupting: S eeing that they are without heart, || N or do they seek for it.”
38.15: And I was made wise so as not to fall into the hands of the deceiver; A nd I congratulated myself because the Truth went with me,
38.16: And I was established, and lived, and was redeemed,
38.17: And my foundations were laid on the hand of the Lord, || B ecause He established me.
38.18: For He set the root, and watered it, and fixed it, and blessed it; A nd its fruits are forever.
38.19: It struck deep, and sprung up, and spread out, and was full and enlarged.
38.20: And the Lord alone was glorified in His planting and in His farming || B y His care and by the blessing of His lips,
38.21: By the beautiful planting of His right hand, || A nd by the discovery of His planting, || A nd by the thought of His mind. Hallelujah!

39.1: Great rivers are the power of the Lord:
39.2: And they carry headlong those who despise Him, || A nd entangle their paths;
39.3: And they sweep away their fords, || A nd catch their bodies, and destroy their lives.
39.4: For they are swifter than lightning and more rapid, || A nd those who cross them in faith are not moved;
39.5: And those who walk on them without blemish will not be afraid.
39.6: For the sign in them is the Lord; A nd the sign is the way of those who cross in the Name of the Lord;
39.7: Therefore put on the Name of the Most High, || A nd know Him and you will cross without danger, || F or the rivers will be subject to you.
39.8: The Lord has bridged them by His word; A nd He walked and crossed them on foot:
39.9: And His footsteps stand firm on the water, || A nd are not injured; T hey are as firm as a tree that is truly set up.
39.10: And the waves were lifted up on this side and on that, || B ut the footsteps of our Lord Messiah stand firm, and are not obliterated, and are not defaced.
39.11: And a way has been appointed for those who cross after Him, and for those who adhere to the course of faith in Him and worship His Name. Hallelujah!

40.1: As the honey distills from the comb of the bees,
40.2: And the milk flows from the woman that loves her children;
40.3: So also is my hope on You, my God.
40.4: As the fountain gushes out its water,
40.5: So my heart gushes out the praise of the Lord, and my lips utter praise to Him, || A nd my tongue His psalms,
40.6: And my face exults with His gladness, and my spirit exults in His love, and my soul shines in Him.
40.7: And reverence confides in Him, || A nd redemption stands assured in Him.
40.8: And His inheritance is immortal life, || A nd those who participate in it are incorruptible. Hallelujah!

41.1: All the Lord ’s children will praise Him, || A nd will collect the truth of His faith.
41.2: And His children will be known to Him. Therefore, we will sing in His love.
41.3: We live in the Lord by His grace, || A nd we receive life in His Messiah.
41.4: For a great day has shined on us: A nd marvelous is He who has given us of His glory.
41.5: Therefore, let all of us unite together in the Name of the Lord, || A nd let us honor Him in His goodness,
41.6: And let our faces shine in His light, || A nd let our hearts meditate in His love by night and by day.
41.7: Let us exult with the joy of the Lord.
41.8: All those that see me will be astonished, || For I am from another race;
41.9: For the Father of truth remembered me—He who possessed me from the beginning.
41.10: For His bounty begot me, || A nd the thought of His heart.
41.11: And His Word is with us in all our ways—
41.12: The Savior who makes alive and does not reject our souls;
41.13: The Man who was humbled, || A nd exalted by His own righteousness.
41.14: The Son of the Most High appeared in the perfection of His Father,
41.15: And light dawned from the Word that was before time in Him.
41.16: The Messiah is truly one; A nd He was known before the foundation of the world,
41.17: That He might save souls forever by the truth of His Name: A new song arises from those who love Him. Hallelujah!

42.1: I stretched out my hands and approached my Lord,
42.2: My expansion is the outspread tree [[ or cross ]], which was set up on the way of the Righteous One.
42.4: And I became of no account to those who did not take hold of Me and I will be with those who love Me.
42.5: All my persecutors are dead; A nd they sought after Me who hoped in Me, || B ecause I was alive.
42.6: And I rose up and am with them; A nd I will speak by their mouths,
42.7: For they have despised those who persecuted them.
42.8: And I lifted up over them the yoke of My love.
42.9: Like the arm of the bridegroom over the bride,
42.10: So was My yoke over those that know Me;
42.11: And as the couch that is spread in the house of the bridegroom and bride,
42.12: So is My love over those that believe in Me.
42.13: Sheol saw Me and was made miserable:
42.14: I did not perish [forever], || T hough they devised it against Me.
42.16: Death cast Me up and many along with Me.
42.17: I had gall and bitterness, || A nd I went down with him to the utmost of his depth:
42.18: And the feet and the head he let go, || F or they were not able to endure My face.
42.19: And I made a congregation of living men among his dead men, || A nd I spoke with them by living lips,
42.20: Because My word will not be void.
42.21: And those who had died ran toward Me, || A nd they cried and said, “Son of God, have pity on us, || A nd do with us according to Your kindness.
42.22: And bring us out from the bonds of darkness, || A nd open to us the door by which we will come out to You.
42.23: For we see that our death has not touched You.
42.24: Let us also be redeemed with You, || F or You are our Redeemer.”
42.25: And I heard their voice, || A nd I sealed My Name on their heads;
42.26: For they are free men, and they are Mine. Hallelujah!

1.1: I, O King, by the grace of God came into this world;
1.2: and when I had considered the heavens, and the earth, and the seas, and had surveyed the sun and the rest of creation,
1.3: I marveled at the beauty of the world.
1.4: And I perceived that the world and all that is therein are moved by the power of another;
1.5: and I understood that He who moves them is God, who is hidden in them, and veiled by them.
1.6: And it is manifest that that which causes motion is more powerful than that which is moved.
1.7: But that I should make search concerning this same Mover of all, as to what is His nature (for it seems to me, He is indeed unsearchable in His nature),
1.8: and that I should argue as to the constancy of His government, so as to grasp it fully—this is a vain effort for me;
1.9: for it is not possible that a man should fully comprehend it.
1.10: I say, however, concerning this Mover of the world, that He is God of all,
1.11: who made all things for the sake of mankind.
1.12: And it seems to me that this is reasonable, that one should fear God and should not oppress man.
1.13: I say, then, that God is not born, not made, an ever-abiding nature without beginning and without end, immortal, perfect, and incomprehensible.
1.14: Now when I say that He is “perfect,” this means that there is not in Him any defect,
1.15: and He is not in need of anything but all things are in need of Him.
1.16: And when I say that He is “without beginning,” this means that everything which has beginning has also an end, and that which has an end may be brought to an end.
1.17: He has no name, for everything which has a name is related to things created.
1.18: He has no form, nor yet any union of members; for whatever possesses these is related to things fashioned.
1.19: He is neither male nor female.
1.20: The heavens do not limit Him, but the heavens and all things, visible and invisible, receive their bounds from Him.
1.21: He has no adversary, for there exists not any stronger than He.
1.22: Wrath and indignation He does not possess, for there is nothing which is able to stand against Him.
1.23: Ignorance and forgetfulness are not in His nature, for He is altogether wisdom and understanding;
1.24: and in Him stands fast all that exists.
1.25: He requires not sacrifice and libation, nor even one of things visible;
1.26: He requires not anything from any, but all living creatures stand in need of Him.

2.1: Since, then, we have addressed you concerning God, so far as our discourse can bear on Him,
2.2: let us now come to the race of men, that we may know which of them participate in the truth of which we have spoken, and which of them go astray from it.
2.3: This is clear to you, O King, that there are four classes of men in this world: Barbarians and Greeks, Jews and Christians.
2.4: The Barbarians, indeed, trace the origin of their kind of religion from Kronos and from Rhea and their other gods;
2.5: the Greeks, however, from Helenos, who is said to be sprung from Zeus.
2.6: And by Helenos there were born Aiolos and Xuthos;
2.7: and there were others descended from Inachos and Phoroneus,
2.8: and lastly from the Egyptian Danaos and from Kadmos and from Dionysos.
2.9: The Jews, again, trace the origin of their race from Abraham, who begot Isaac, of whom was born Jacob.
2.10: And he begot twelve sons who migrated from Syria to Egypt;
2.11: and there they were called the nation of the Hebrews, by him who made their laws;
2.12: and at length they were named Jews.
2.13: The Christians, then, trace the beginning of their religion from Jesus the Messiah;
2.14: and He is named the Son of God Most High.
2.15: And it is said that God came down from Heaven,
2.16: and from a Hebrew virgin assumed and clothed Himself with flesh;
2.17: and the Son of God lived in a daughter of man.
2.18: This is taught in the Gospel, as it is called, which a short time was preached among them;
2.19: and you also if you will read therein, may perceive the power which belongs to it.
2.20: This Jesus, then, was born of the race of the Hebrews;
2.21: and He had twelve disciples in order that the purpose of His incarnation might in time be accomplished.
2.22: But He Himself was pierced by the Jews, and He died and was buried;
2.23: and they say that after three days He rose and [later] ascended to Heaven.
2.24: Thereon these twelve disciples went forth throughout the known parts of the world and kept showing His greatness with all modesty and uprightness.
2.25: And hence also those of the present day who believe that preaching are called Christians, and they have become famous.
2.26: So then there are, as I said above, four classes of men: Barbarians and Greeks, Jews and Christians.
2.27: Moreover the wind is obedient to God, and fire to the messengers; the waters also to the demons and the earth to the sons of men.

3.1: Let us begin, then, with the Barbarians, and go on to the rest of the nations one after another,
3.2: that we may see which of them hold the truth as to God and which of them hold error.
3.3: The Barbarians, then, as they did not apprehend God, went astray among the elements,
3.4: and began to worship things created instead of their Creator;
3.5: and for this end they made images and shut them up in shrines, and behold, they worship them,
3.6: guarding them the while with much care, lest their gods be stolen by robbers.
3.7: And the Barbarians did not observe that that which acts as guard is greater than that which is guarded,
3.8: and that everyone who creates is greater than that which is created.
3.9: If it be, then, that their gods are too feeble to see to their own safety, how will they take thought for the safety of men?
3.10: Great then is the error into which the Barbarians wandered in worshiping lifeless images which can do nothing to help them.
3.11: And I am led to wonder, O King, at their philosophers, how that even they went astray, and gave the name of gods to images which were made in honor of the elements;
3.12: and that their sages did not perceive that the elements are also dissoluble and perishable.
3.13: For if a small part of an element is dissolved or destroyed, the whole of it may be dissolved and destroyed.
3.14: If then the elements themselves are dissolved and destroyed and forced to be subject to another that is more stubborn than they,
3.15: and if they are not in their nature gods, why, indeed, do they call the images which are made in their honor, God?
3.16: Great, then, is the error which the philosophers among them have brought on their followers.

4.1: Let us turn now, O King, to the elements in themselves,
4.2: that we may make clear in regard to them, that they are not gods, but a created thing, liable to ruin and change, which is of the same nature as man;
4.3: whereas God is imperishable and unvarying, and invisible, while yet He sees, and overrules, and transforms all things.
4.4: Those then who believe concerning the earth that it is a god have until now deceived themselves,
4.5: since it is furrowed and set with plants and trenched;
4.6: and it takes in the filthy refuse of men and beasts and cattle.
4.7: And at times it becomes unfruitful, for if it be burned to ashes it becomes devoid of life,
4.8: for nothing germinates from an earthen jar.
4.9: And besides if water be collected on it, it is dissolved together with its products.
4.10: And behold, it is trodden under foot of men and beast, and receives the blood of the slain;
4.11: and it is dug open, and filled with the dead, and becomes a tomb for corpses.
4.12: But it is impossible that a nature, which is holy and worthy and blessed and immortal, should allow of any one of these things.
4.13: And hence it appears to us that the earth is not a god but a creation of God.

5.1: In the same way, again, those erred who believed the waters to be gods.
5.2: For the waters were created for the use of man, and are put under his rule in many ways.
5.3: For they suffer change and admit impurity, and are destroyed and lose their nature while they are boiled into many substances.
5.4: And they take colors which do not belong to them;
5.5: they are also congealed by frost and are mingled and permeated with the filth of men and beasts, and with the blood of the slain.
5.6: And being checked by skilled workmen through the restraint of aqueducts, they flow and are diverted against their inclination,
5.7: and come into gardens and other places in order that they may be collected and issue forth as a means of fertility for man,
5.8: and that they may cleanse away every impurity and fulfill the service man requires from them.
5.9: For what reason it is impossible that the waters should be a god, but they are a work of God and a part of the world.
5.10: In like manner also they who believed that fire is a god erred to no slight extent.
5.11: For it, too, was created for the service of men, and is subject to them in many ways: in the preparation of meats, and as a means of casting metals, and for other ends of which your Majesty is aware.
5.12: At the same time it is quenched and extinguished in many ways.
5.13: Again they also erred who believed the motion of the winds to be a god.
5.14: For it is well known to us that those winds are under the dominion of another,
5.15: at times their motion increases, and at times it fails and ceases at the command of Him who controls them.
5.16: For they were created by God for the sake of men, in order to supply the necessity of trees and fruits and seeds;
5.17: and to bring over the sea ships which transport for men necessities and goods from places where they are found to places where they are not found;
5.18: and to govern the quarters of the world.
5.19: And as for itself, at times it increases and again abates;
5.20: and in one place brings help and in another causes disaster at the bidding of Him who rules it.
5.21: And mankind is also able by known means to confine and keep it in check in order that it may fulfill for them the service they require from it.
5.22: And of itself it does not have any authority at all.
5.23: And hence it is impossible that the winds should be called gods, but rather a thing made by God.

6.1: So also, they erred who believed that the sun is a god.
6.2: For we see that it is moved by the compulsion of another, and revolves and makes its journey,
6.3: and proceeds from sign to sign, rising and setting every day, so as to give warmth for the growth of plants and trees,
6.4: and to bring forth into the air with which it [(sunlight)] is mingled every growing thing which is on the earth.
6.5: And to it there belongs by comparison a part in common with the rest of the stars in its course;
6.6: and though it is one in its nature it is associated with many parts for the supply of the needs of men;
6.7: and that not according to its own will but rather according to the will of Him who rules it.
6.8: And hence it is impossible that the sun should be a god, but the work of God;
6.9: and in like manner also the moon and the stars.

7.1: And those who believed of the men of the past, that some of them were gods, they too were much mistaken.
7.2: For as you yourself allow, O King, man is constituted of the four elements, and of a soul and a spirit (and hence he is called a microcosm),
7.3: and without any one of these parts he could not consist.
7.4: He has a beginning and an end, and he is born and dies.
7.5: But God, as I said, has none of these things in His nature, but is uncreated and imperishable.
7.6: And hence it is not possible that we should set up man to be of the nature of God—
7.7: man, to whom at times when he looks for joy, there comes trouble, and when he looks for laughter there comes to him weeping,
7.8: who is wrathful and covetous and envious, with other defects as well.
7.9: And he is destroyed in many ways by the elements and also by the animals.
7.10: And hence, O King, we are bound to recognize the error of the Barbarians,
7.11: that thereby, since they did not find traces of the true God, they fell aside from the truth, and went after the desire of their imagination,
7.12: serving the perishable elements and lifeless images, and through their error not apprehending what the true God is.

8.1: Let us turn further to the Greeks also, that we may know what opinion they hold as to the true God.
8.2: The Greeks, then, because they are more subtle than the Barbarians, have gone further astray than the Barbarians;
8.3: inasmuch as they have introduced many fictitious gods, and have set up some of them as males and some as females;
8.4: and in that some of their gods were found who were adulterers, and did murder, and were deluded, and envious, and wrathful and passionate, and parricides, and thieves, and robbers.
8.5: And some of them, they say, were crippled and limped, and some were sorcerers, and some actually went mad, and some played on lyres, and some were given to roaming on the hills, and some even died, and some were struck dead by lightning, and some were made servants even to men, and some escaped by flight, and some were kidnapped by men, and some, indeed, were lamented and deplored by men.
8.6: And some, they say, went down to Sheol, and some were grievously wounded, and some transformed themselves into the likeness of animals to seduce the race of mortal women, and some polluted themselves by lying with males.
8.7: And some, they say, were wedded to their mothers and their sisters and their daughters.
8.8: And they say of their gods that they committed adultery with the daughters of men;
8.9: and of these there was born a certain race which also was mortal.
8.10: And they say that some of the females disputed about beauty and appeared before men for judgment.
8.11: Thus, O King, have the Greeks put forward foulness, and absurdity, and folly about their gods and about themselves, in that they have called those that are of such a nature gods, who are not gods.
8.12: And hence mankind has received incitements to commit adultery and fornication, and to steal and to practice all that is offensive and hated and abhorred.
8.13: For if they who are called their gods practiced all these things which are written above, how much more should men practice them—
8.14: men, who believe that their gods themselves practiced them.
8.15: And owing to the foulness of this error there have happened to mankind harassing wars, and great famines, and bitter captivity, and complete desolation.
8.16: And behold, it was by reason of this alone that they suffered and that all these things came on them;
8.17: and while they endured those things they did not perceive in their mind that for their error those things came on them.

9.1: Let us proceed further to their account of their gods that we may carefully demonstrate all that is said above.
9.2: First of all, the Greeks bring forward as a god Kronos, that is to say Chiun [(Saturn)].
9.3: And his worshipers sacrifice their children to him, and they burn some of them alive in his honor.
9.4: And they say that he took to himself among his wives Rhea, and begot many children by her.
9.5: By her too he begot Dios, who is called Zeus.
9.6: And at length he [(Kronos)] went mad, and through fear of an oracle that had been made known to him, he began to devour his sons.
9.7: And from him Zeus was stolen away without his knowledge;
9.8: and at length Zeus bound him, and mutilated the signs of his manhood, and flung them into the sea.
9.9: And hence, as they say in a fable, there was engendered Aphrodite, who is called Astarte.
9.10: And he [(Zeus)] cast out Kronos bound into darkness.
9.11: Great then is the error and ignominy which the Greeks have brought forward about the first of their gods,
9.12: in that they have said all this about him, O King.
9.13: It is impossible that a god should be bound or mutilated; and if it be otherwise, he is indeed miserable.
9.14: And after Kronos they bring forward another god Zeus.
9.15: And they say of him that he assumed the sovereignty and was king over all the gods.
9.16: And they say that he changed himself into a beast and other shapes in order to seduce mortal women, and to raise up by them children for himself.
9.17: Once, they say, he changed himself into a bull through love of Europe and Pasiphae.
9.18: And again he changed himself into the likeness of gold through love of Danae, and to a swan through love of Leda, and to a man through love of Antiope, and to lightning through love of Luna,
9.19: and so by these he begot many children.
9.20: For by Antiope, they say, that he begot Zethus and Amphion, and by Luna Dionysos, by Alcmena Hercules,
9.21: and by Leto, Apollo and Artemis, and by Danae Perseus, and by Leda, Castor and Polydeuces, and Helene and Paludus,
9.22: and by Mnemosyne he begot nine daughters whom they styled the Muses,
9.23: and by Europe, Minos and Rhadamanthos and Sarpedon.
9.24: And lastly, he changed himself into the likeness of an eagle through his passion for Ganydemos [(Ganymede)] the shepherd.
9.25: By reason of these tales, O King, much evil has arisen among men, who to this day are imitators of their gods,
9.26: and practice adultery and defile themselves with their mothers and their sisters,
9.28: and some make bold to slay even their parents.
9.29: For if he who is said to be the chief and king of their gods does these things, how much more should his worshipers imitate him?
9.30: And great is the folly which the Greeks have brought forward in their narrative concerning him.
9.31: For it is impossible that a god should practice adultery or fornication or come near to lie with males or kill his parents;
9.32: and if it be otherwise, he is much worse than a destructive demon.

10.1: Again they bring forward as another god Hephaistos.
10.2: And they say of him, that he is lame, and a cap is set on his head, and he holds in his hands firetongs and a hammer;
10.3: and he follows the craft of iron working, that thereby he may procure the necessities of his livelihood.
10.4: Is then this god so very needy?
10.5: But it cannot be that a god should be needy or lame, else he is very worthless.
10.6: And further they bring in another god and call him Hermes.
10.7: And they say that he is a thief, a lover of wealth, and greedy for gain, and a magician, and mutilated, and an athlete, and an interpreter of language.
10.8: But it is impossible that a god should be a magician, or greedy, or maimed, or craving for what is not his, or an athlete.
10.9: And if it be otherwise, he is found to be useless.
10.10: And after him they bring forward as another god Asclepius.
10.11: And they say that he is a physician and prepares drugs and plaster that he may supply the necessities of his livelihood.
10.12: Is then this god in want?
10.13: And at length he was struck with lightning by Dios on account of Tyndareos of Lacedaemon, and so he died.
10.14: If then Asclepius were a god, and, when he was struck with lightning, was unable to help himself, how should he be able to give help to others?
10.15: But that a divine nature should be in want or be destroyed by lightning is impossible.
10.16: And again they bring forward another as a god, and they call him Ares.
10.17: And they say that he is a warrior, and jealous, and covets sheep and things which are not his.
10.18: And he makes gain by his arms.
10.19: And they say that at length he committed adultery with Aphrodite, and was caught by the little boy Eros and by Hephaistos the husband of Aphrodite.
10.20: But it is impossible that a god should be a warrior or bound or an adulterer.
10.21: And again they say of Dionysos that he, indeed, is a god, who arranges carousals by night, and teaches drunkenness, and carries off women who do not belong to him.
10.22: And at length, they say, he went mad and dismissed his handmaidens and fled into the desert; and during his madness he ate serpents.
10.23: And at last he was killed by Titanos.
10.24: If then Dionysos were a god, and when he was being killed was unable to help himself, how is it possible that he should help others?
10.25: Herakles next they bring forward and say that he is a god, who hates detestable things, a tyrant, and warrior and a destroyer of plagues.
10.26: And of him also they say that at length he became mad and killed his own children, and cast himself into a fire and died.
10.27: If then Herakles is a god, and in all these calamities was unable to rescue himself, how should others ask help from him?
10.28: But it is impossible that a god should be mad, or drunken, or a slayer of his children, or consumed by fire.

11.1: And after him they bring forward another god and call him Apollo.
11.2: And they say that he is jealous and inconstant, and at times he holds the bow and quiver, and again the lyre and plectron.
11.3: And he utters oracles for men that he may receive rewards from them.
11.4: Is then this god in need of rewards?
11.5: But it is an insult that all these things should be found with a god.
11.6: And after him they bring forward as a goddess Artemis, the sister of Apollo;
11.7: and they say that she was a huntress and that she herself used to carry a bow and bolts, and to roam about on the mountains, leading the hounds to hunt stags or wild bears of the field.
11.8: But it is disgraceful that a virgin maid should roam alone on the hills or hunt in the chase for animals.
11.9: For what reason it is impossible that Artemis should be a goddess.
11.10: Again they say of Aphrodite that she indeed is a goddess.
11.11: And at times she dwells with their gods, but at other times she is a neighbor to men.
11.12: And once she had Ares as a lover, and again Adonis who is Tammuz.
11.13: Once also, Aphrodite was wailing and weeping for the death of Tammuz, and they say that she went down to Sheol that she might redeem Adonis from Persephone, who is the daughter of Sheol [(Hades)].
11.14: If then Aphrodite is a goddess and was unable to help her lover at his death, how will she find it possible to help others?
11.15: And this cannot be listened to, that a divine nature should come to weeping and wailing and adultery.
11.16: And again they say of Tammuz that he is a god.
11.17: And he is, indeed, a hunter and an adulterer.
11.18: And they say that he was killed by a wound from a wild boar, without being able to help himself.
11.19: And if he could not help himself, how can he take thought for the human race?
11.20: But that a god should be an adulterer or a hunter or should die by violence is impossible.
11.21: Again they say of Rhea that she is the mother of their gods.
11.22: And they say that she had once a lover Atys, and that she used to delight in depraved men.
11.23: And at last she raised a lamentation and mourned for Atys her lover.
11.24: If then the mother of their gods was unable to help her lover and deliver him from death, how can she help others?
11.25: So it is disgraceful that a goddess should lament and weep and take delight in depraved men.
11.26: Again they introduce Kore and say that she is a goddess, and she was stolen away by Pluto, and could not help herself.
11.27: If then she is a goddess and was unable to help herself how will she find means to help others?
11.28: For a god who is stolen away is very powerless.
11.29: All this, then, O King, have the Greeks brought forward concerning their gods,
11.30: and they have invented and declared it concerning them.
11.31: And hence all men received an impulse to work all profanity and all defilements; and hereby the whole earth was corrupted.

12.1: The Egyptians, moreover, because they are more base and stupid than every people that is on the earth, have themselves erred more than all.
12.2: For the deities [[ or religion ]] of the Barbarians and the Greeks did not suffice for them, but they introduced some also of the nature of the animals, and said thereof that they were gods,
12.3: and likewise of creeping things which are found on the dry land and in the waters.
12.4: And of plants and herbs they said that some of them were gods.
12.5: And they were corrupted by every kind of delusion and defilement more than every people that is on the earth.
12.6: For from ancient times they worshiped Isis, and they say that she is a goddess whose husband was Osiris her brother.
12.7: And when Osiris was killed by Typhon his brother, Isis fled with Horos her son to Byblos in Syria, and was there for a certain time until her son was grown.
12.8: And he contended with Typhon his uncle, and killed him.
12.9: And then Isis returned and went around with Horos her son and sought for the dead body of Osiris her lord, bitterly lamenting his death.
12.10: If then Isis be a goddess, and could not help Osiris her brother and lord, how can she help another?
12.11: But it is impossible that a divine nature should be afraid, and flee for safety, or should weep and wail; or else it is very miserable.
12.12: And of Osiris also they say that he is a serviceable god.
12.13: And he was killed by Typhon and was unable to help himself.
12.14: But it is well known that this cannot be asserted of divinity.
12.15: And further, they say of his brother Typhon that he is a god, who killed his brother and was killed by his brother’s son and by his bride, being unable to help himself.
12.16: And how, pray, is he a god who does not save himself?
12.17: As the Egyptians, then, were more stupid than the rest of the nations, these and such like gods did not suffice for them.
12.18: No, but they even apply the name of gods to animals in which there is no soul at all.
12.19: For some of them worship the sheep and others the calf;
12.20: and some the pig and others the shad fish;
12.21: and some the crocodile, and the hawk, and the fish, and the ibis, and the vulture, and the eagle, and the raven.
12.22: Some of them worship the cat, and others the turbot-fish, some the dog, some the adder, and some the asp, and others the lion;
12.23: and others the garlic, and onions, and thorns,
12.24: and others the tiger and other such things.
12.25: And the poor creatures do not see that all these things are nothing,
12.26: although they daily witness their gods being eaten and consumed by men and also by their fellows;
12.27: while some of them are cremated, and some die and decay and become dust, without their observing that they perish in many ways.
12.28: So the Egyptians have not observed that such things which are not equal to their own deliverance, are not gods.
12.29: And if, indeed, they are weak in the case of their own deliverance, from where have they power to help in the case of deliverance of their worshipers?
12.30: Great then is the error into which the Egyptians wandered—greater, indeed, than that of any people which is on the face of the earth.

13.1: But it is a marvel, O King, with regard to the Greeks, who surpass all other peoples in their manner of life and reasoning, how they have gone astray after dead idols and lifeless images.
13.2: And yet they see their gods in the hands of their craftsmen being sawn out, and planed and docked, and hacked short, and charred, and ornamented, and being altered by them in every kind of way.
13.3: And when they grow old, and are worn away through lapse of time, and when they are molten and crushed to powder, how, I wonder, did they not perceive concerning them, that they are not gods?
13.4: And as for those who did not find deliverance for themselves, how can they serve the distress of men?
13.5: But even the writers and philosophers among them have wrongly alleged that the gods are such as are made in honor of God Almighty.
13.6: And they err in seeking to liken [them] to God whom man has not at any time seen nor can see to what He is like.
13.7: Herein too [they err] in asserting of deity that any such thing as deficiency can be present to it;
13.8: as when they say that He receives sacrifice and requires burnt-offering and libation and immolations of men, and temples.
13.9: But God is not in need, and none of these things is necessary to Him;
13.10: and it is clear that men err in these things they imagine.
13.11: Further their writers and their philosophers represent and declare that the nature of all their gods is one.
13.12: And they have not apprehended God our Lord who while He is one, is in all. They err therefore.
13.13: For if the body of a man while it is many in its parts is not in dread, one member of another, but, since it is a united body, wholly agrees with itself; even so also God is one in His nature.
13.14: A single essence is proper to Him, since He is uniform in His nature and His essence;
13.15: and He is not afraid of Himself.
13.16: If then the nature of the gods is one, it is not proper that a god should either pursue or slay or harm a god.
13.17: If then gods be pursued and wounded by gods, and some be kidnapped, and some struck dead by lightning, it is obvious that the nature of their gods is not one.
13.18: And hence it is known, O King, that it is a mistake when they reckon and bring the natures of their gods under a single nature.
13.19: If then it becomes us to admire a god which is seen and does not see,
13.20: how much more praiseworthy is it that one should believe in a nature which is invisible and all-seeing?
13.21: And if further it is fitting that one should approve the handiworks of a craftsman, how much more is it fitting that one should glorify the Creator of the craftsman?
13.22: For behold, when the Greeks made laws, they did not perceive that by their laws they condemn their gods.
13.23: For if their laws are righteous, their gods are unrighteous, since they transgressed the law in killing one another, and practicing sorcery, and committing adultery, and in robbing and stealing, and in lying with males, and by their other practices as well.
13.24: For if their gods were right in doing all these things as they are described, then the laws of the Greeks are unrighteous in not being made according to the will of their gods.
13.25: And in that case the whole world is gone astray.
13.26: For the narratives about their gods are some of them myths, and some of them nature-poems, and some of them hymns and elegies.
13.27: The hymns indeed and elegies are empty words and noise.
13.28: But these nature-poems, even if they are made as they say, still those are not gods who do such things and suffer and endure such things.
13.29: And those myths are shallow tales with no depth whatever in them.

14.1: Let us come now, O King, to the history of the Jews also, and see what opinion they have as to God.
14.2: The Jews then say that God is one, the Creator of all, and omnipotent;
14.3: and that it is not right that any other should be worshiped except this God alone.
14.4: And herein they appear to approach the truth more than all the nations, especially in that they worship God and not His works.
14.5: And they imitate God by the philanthropy which prevails among them;
14.6: for they have compassion on the poor, and they release the captives, and bury the dead, and do such things as these, which are acceptable before God and well-pleasing also to men,
14.7: which they have received from their forefathers.
14.8: Nevertheless, they too erred from true knowledge.
14.9: And in their imagination, they conceive that it is God they serve;
14.10: whereas by their mode of observance it is to the messengers and not to God that their service is rendered—
14.11: as when they celebrate Sabbaths and the beginning of the months, and feasts of unleavened bread, and a great fast;
14.12: and fasting, and circumcision, and the purification of meats, which things, however, they do not observe perfectly.

15.1: But the Christians, O King, while they went around and made search, have found the truth;
15.2: and as we learned from their writings, they have come nearer to truth and genuine knowledge than the rest of the nations.
15.3: For they know and trust in God, the Creator of Heaven and of earth, in whom and from whom are all things, to whom there is no other god as companion,
15.4: from whom they received commands which they engraved on their minds and observe in hope and expectation of the world which is to come.
15.5: For what reason they do not commit adultery nor fornication, nor bear false witness, nor embezzle what is held in pledge, nor covet what is not theirs.
15.6: They honor father and mother, and show kindness to those near to them; and whenever they are judges, they judge uprightly.
15.7: They do not worship idols in the image of man;
15.8: and whatever they would not that others should do to them, they do not do to others;
15.9: and of the food which is consecrated to idols they do not eat, for they are pure.
15.10: And their oppressors they comfort and make them their friends; they do good to their enemies;
15.11: and their women, O King, are pure as virgins, and their daughters are modest;
15.12: and their men keep themselves from every unlawful union and from all uncleanness, in the hope of a reward to come in the other world.
15.13: Further, if one or other of them have bondmen and bondwomen, or children, through love toward them they persuade them to become Christians,
15.14: and when they have done so, they call them brothers without distinction.
15.15: They do not worship strange gods, and they go their way in all modesty and cheerfulness.
15.16: Falsehood is not found among them; and they love one another, and from widows they do not turn away their esteem;
15.17: and they deliver the orphan from him who treats him harshly.
15.18: And he who has, gives to him who does not have, without boasting.
15.19: And when they see a stranger, they take him into their homes and rejoice over him as a very brother;
15.20: for they do not call them brothers after the flesh, but brothers after the spirit and in God.
15.21: And whenever one of their poor passes from the world, each one of them according to his ability gives heed to him and carefully sees to his burial.
15.22: And if they hear that one of their number is imprisoned or afflicted on account of the name of their Messiah, all of them anxiously minister to his necessity, and if it is possible to redeem him they set him free.
15.23: And if there is among them any that is poor and needy, and if they have no spare food, they fast two or three days in order to supply to the needy their lack of food.
15.24: They observe the precepts of their Messiah with much care, living justly and soberly as the Lord their God commanded them.
15.25: Every morning and every hour they give thanks and praise to God for His loving-kindnesses toward them;
15.26: and for their food and their drink they offer thanksgiving to Him.
15.27: And if any righteous man among them passes from the world, they rejoice and offer thanks to God; and they escort his body as if he were setting out from one place to another near.
15.28: And when a child has been born to one of them, they give thanks to God;
15.29: and if moreover it happens to die in childhood, they give thanks to God the more, as for one who has passed through the world without sins.
15.30: And further if they see that any one of them dies in his ungodliness or in his sins, for him they grieve bitterly, and sorrow as for one who goes to meet his doom.

16.1: Such, O King, is the command of the law of the Christians, and such is their manner of life.
16.2: As men who know God, they ask from Him petitions which are fitting for Him to grant and for them to receive.
16.3: And thus they employ their whole lifetime.
16.4: And since they know the loving-kindnesses of God toward them, behold, for their sake the glorious things which are in the world flow forth to view.
16.5: And truly, they are those who found the truth when they went around and made search for it;
16.6: and from what we considered, we learned that they alone come near to a knowledge of the truth.
16.7: And they do not proclaim in the ears of the multitude the kind deeds they do, but are careful that no one should notice them;
16.8: and they conceal their giving just as he who finds a treasure and conceals it.
16.9: And they strive to be righteous as those who expect to behold their Messiah, and to receive from Him with great glory the promises made concerning them.
16.10: And as for their words and their precepts, O King, and their glorying in their worship, and the hope of earning according to the work of each one of them their reward which they look for in another world, you may learn about these from their writings.
16.11: It is enough for us to have shortly informed your Majesty concerning the conduct and the truth of the Christians.
16.12: For great indeed, and wonderful, is their doctrine to him who will search into it and reflect on it.
16.13: And truly, this is a new people, and there is a divine admixture in the midst of them.
16.14: Take, then, their writings, and read therein, and behold, you will find that I have not put forth these things on my own authority, nor spoken thus as their advocate;
16.15: but since I read in their writings I was fully assured of these things as also of things which are to come.
16.16: And for this reason I was constrained to declare the truth to such as care for it and seek the world to come.
16.17: And to me there is no doubt but that the earth abides through the supplication of the Christians.
16.18: But the rest of the nations err and cause error in wallowing before the elements of the world, since beyond these their mental vision will not pass.
16.19: And they search about as if in darkness because they will not recognize the truth;
16.20: and like drunken men they reel and jostle one another and fall.

17.1: Thus far, O King, I have spoken;
17.2: for concerning that which remains, as is said above, there are found in their other writings things which are hard to utter and difficult for one to narrate,
17.3: which are not only spoken in words but also worked out in deeds.
17.4: Now the Greeks, O King, as they follow base practices in intercourse with males, and a mother and a sister and a daughter, impute their monstrous impurity in turn to the Christians.
17.5: But the Christians are just and good, and the truth is set before their eyes, and their spirit is long-suffering;
17.6: and, therefore, though they know the error of these [(the Greeks)], and are persecuted by them, they bear and endure it;
17.7: and for the most part they have compassion on them, as men who are destitute of knowledge.
17.8: And on their side, they offer prayer that these may convert from their error;
17.9: and when it happens that one of them has converted, he is ashamed before the Christians of the works which were done by him;
17.10: and he makes confession to God, saying, “I did these things in ignorance.”
17.11: And he purifies his heart, and his sins are forgiven him,
17.12: because he committed them in ignorance in the former time, when he used to blaspheme and speak evil of the true knowledge of the Christians.
17.13: And assuredly the race of the Christians is more blessed than all the men who are on the face of the earth.
17.14: From now on let the tongues of those who utter vanity and harass the Christians be silent;
17.15: and hereafter let them speak the truth.
17.16: For it is of serious consequence to them that they should worship the true God rather than worship a senseless sound.
17.17: And truly whatever is spoken in the mouth of the Christians is of God; and their doctrine is the gateway of light.
17.18: For what reason let all who are without the knowledge of God draw near to that;
17.19: and they will receive incorruptible words, which are from all time and forever.
17.20: So they will appear before the awful judgment which through Jesus the Messiah is destined to come on the whole human race. · THE END OF THE APOCRYPHA ·
